facts.text: The oldest known evidence of bread making comes from a 14,000-year-old site in northeastern Jordan, showing bread predates farming.
Cheese making is thousands of years old, and some of the earliest direct evidence was found in ancient pottery in Poland.
Rice is a staple food for more than half of the world's population.
Tomatoes originated in western South America before spreading to Europe and the rest of the world.
Potatoes were first domesticated in the Andes Mountains of South America.
Pizza Margherita is traditionally said to have been named for Queen Margherita of Italy in 1889.
The sandwich is named after John Montagu, the 4th Earl of Sandwich.
Chocolate was first consumed in Mesoamerica as a bitter drink made from cacao.
According to legend, coffee was discovered in Ethiopia after goats became energetic from eating coffee cherries.
Tea culture began in ancient China and later spread across Asia, Europe, and beyond.
Sushi began as a method of preserving fish with fermented rice before becoming the fresh dish many people know today.
Yogurt is made by fermenting milk with live bacterial cultures.
There are hundreds of varieties of kimchi in Korean cuisine.
MSG was isolated in 1908 by Japanese chemist Kikunae Ikeda, who identified umami as a distinct savory taste.
The Scoville scale measures the heat of chili peppers and spicy foods.
Vanilla comes from the seed pods of orchids, making it one of the few widely used spices from a flowering orchid.
Honey can last for an extremely long time when sealed properly because of its low moisture and acidic chemistry.
Olive oil is one of the signature ingredients of the Mediterranean diet.
Bananas are berries in botanical terms, while strawberries are not.
Strawberries are considered aggregate accessory fruits rather than true berries.
Peanuts are legumes, which means they are more closely related to beans than to tree nuts.
The Maillard reaction is the browning process that gives roasted, baked, and seared foods much of their flavor.
Umami is recognized as the fifth basic taste alongside sweet, sour, salty, and bitter.
Canning was developed by Nicolas Appert in the early 1800s and revolutionized food preservation.
One of the earliest pressure-cooking devices was invented by Denis Papin in the 17th century.
Chopsticks have been used in China for more than 3,000 years.
Tofu has been made in China for over 2,000 years and became a major protein source across East Asia.
Popcorn has been eaten in the Americas for thousands of years.
Caesar salad was created in Tijuana, Mexico, not in Italy.
Nutmeg was once so valuable in global trade that control of its source islands sparked intense colonial rivalry.
Bread has been one of the world's foundational foods for thousands of years.
Archaeological evidence suggests people were making bread long before the rise of large farming societies.
Bread is one of the standout traditions or topics associated with Food.
Readers and travelers interested in Food often encounter Bread very quickly.
Learning about Bread helps explain part of the appeal and diversity of Food.
For many people around the world, Bread is a memorable part of Food.
Documentaries, articles, and travel guides about Food frequently highlight Bread.
The story of Bread connects directly to the broader traditions behind Food.
Bread is frequently used to introduce newcomers to the richness of Food.
Many people associate Food with cultural touchstones such as Bread.
One reason Food feels so vivid and memorable is the presence of traditions and foods such as Bread.
From heritage to everyday life, Bread plays a notable role in how Food is experienced and described.
